content.summary:
The pipeline-autonomy-hardening project aims to make the Lead Engineer + auto-mode pipeline fully autonomous and self-healing, from backlog intake through deployment, with zero human intervention. An audit surfaced 86 findings across five domains: state machine correctness, concurrency/thread safety, security hardening, error handling/self-healing, and test coverage.

The codebase operates three independent finite state machines — Feature FSM (8 states), Pipeline Phase FSM (9 phases), and Ceremony FSM (5 phases) [1][16][19] — each with separate transition rules but no shared validation layer. The most critical systemic risks are: (a) missing transition-matrix enforcement allowing invalid state jumps [2], (b) unbounded retry/polling loops that can stall features indefinitely [3][4], (c) in-memory-only circuit-breaker and pause state that is lost on restart [5], (d) pervasive fire-and-forget error swallowing that creates silent failures [29][30][31], and (e) no recovery path for `interrupted` features after server shutdown [10]. The existing infrastructure — circuit breakers, exponential backoff with jitter, scheduler self-healing, OTEL observability, and Grafana alerting — provides a strong foundation, but the gaps between these components leave the pipeline vulnerable to indefinite stalls, data loss, and undetectable failures.

---
